questions.text: The average gap for Germanium at OK is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This value is typical for the bandgap of silicon, not germanium.
This value is too small for a typical semiconductor bandgap.
The correct answer is c) 6.72 eV because it accurately represents the bandgap energy of germanium.
This value is too large for the bandgap of germanium.

questions.explanation: The correct answer is c) 6.72 eV because it accurately represents the bandgap energy of germanium. The bandgap energy is a critical parameter in semiconductors, representing the energy difference between the valence band and the conduction band. In the case of germanium, the bandgap energy is approximately 0.72 eV. This value is important for understanding the electronic properties of germanium-based devices, such as transistors and diodes, as it dictates their conductivity and optical properties.

questions.text: The process of doping causes the resistivity of semi conductor to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Doping introduces impurities into a semiconductor, which can increase the number of charge carriers (either electrons or holes) available for conduction. This increase in charge carriers results in a decrease in resistivity, as there are more carriers available to carry current through the material.
Doping alters the semiconductor's properties, so its resistivity typically changes rather than remaining constant.
This is incorrect because doping typically decreases the resistivity by increasing the number of charge carriers available for conduction.

questions.explanation: The purpose of doping is to increase the number of free electrons or holes in the semiconductor crystal. It increases the electrical conductivity of the semiconductor. In other words it decreases the resistance of the semiconductor﻿Due to doping of pure semiconductor with trivalent or pentavalent atoms, number density of majority charge carriers increases which increases the conductivity of semiconductor and hence decreases its resistivity
hints: causes the resistivity of semi conductor to

questions.text: Depletion region has:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: The depletion region of a p-n junction contains neither free electrons nor holes. It is an area depleted of mobile charge carriers due to the diffusion of majority carriers from one side to the other. This creates a region with immobile ionized donor and acceptor atoms, resulting in a fixed internal electric field.Depletion region or depletion layer is a region in a P-N junction diode where no mobile charge carriers are present.

questions.text: An n-type substance is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: p-type and n-type semiconductors are formed when impurity atoms, which are themselves electrically neutral, are added to a pure semiconductor that is also electrically neutral. Therefore, the p- or n-type semiconductor formed is electrically neutral.

questions.text: Holes exist in:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: There is no hole current in conductorsbecause they have overlappingvalence and conduction bands. In conductors, electrons are loosely bound to the nucleus hence, can detach easily at room temperature. Also, a large number of free electrons thus, available are conduction electrons

Holes are formed when electrons in atoms move out of the valence band (the outermost shell of the atom that is completely filled with electrons) into the conduction band (the area in an atom where electrons can escape easily), which happens everywhere in a semiconductor.
Holes donot exist in insulators as they donot have free moving electrons
Holes donot exist in conductors or Insulators.

questions.explanation: Holes are formed when electrons in atoms move out of the valence band (the outermost shell of the atom that is completely filled with electrons) into the conduction band (the area in an atom where electrons can escape easily), which happens everywhere in a semiconductor.

questions.text: A pn junction can not be used as:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: p-n junction is also known as a diode.It is a device that is composed of p-type and n-type semiconductors.Its half part is made up of p-type semiconductor and another half part is made of n-type semiconductor.Its working principle is based on forward biasing and reverse biasing.It has various applications like it is used in making rectifiers, which converts A.C. current into D.C. current.It is used in making LEDs ( light-emitting devices).Its working principle is also used in making detectors.But, it is not used in making amplifiers, because the p-n junction does not increase or decrease the input signal and it does not amplify the signal.

questions.text: Hole is equivalent to:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In an applied electric field, the electrons move in one direction, corresponding to the hole moving in the other. If a hole associates itself with a neutral atom, that atom loses an electron and becomes positive. Therefore, the hole is taken to have positive charge of +e, precisely the opposite of the electron charge.The charge on hole is positive. Hence, the charge on a hole is equal to the charge of proton.

questions.text: There is no current due to holes in good electrical conductors because they have:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: When a covalent bond breaks, then the electrons get freed from an atom. This electron moves from the valence band to the conduction band leaving behind a vacancy. This vacancy is called a hole. In case of the conductors, the electrons are loosely bound to the valence band. And they can easily detach from the atoms at the room temperature. So these large numbers of free electrons become available for conduction.An electron moves forward leaving behind a hole. Then another electron captures that hole and moves forward. Thus we can say the hole moves in the opposite direction than the electron and makes up the hole current.In case of conductors, the valence band and the conduction bands overlap. So there are no holes in the conduction band to carry the hole current.Hence there is no hole current in conductors as there is an overlapping of the valence and the conduction bands.

questions.text: Silicon is the most commonly used:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Silicon, a very common element, is used as the raw material of semiconductors because of its stable structure.Silicon is also less expensive as compared to other elements that may be suitable to make semi conductors. Being a semiconductor, the element is put into use for making transistors. Silicon is widely used in computer chips and solar cells. It is a vital component of Portland cement. Silicon is used in the production of fire bricks.

questions.text: The crystal of Germinium or silicon in its pure form at absolute zero acts as:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In silicon, the crystalline form is a silvery, metallic-looking substance. In a silicon lattice, all silicon atoms bond perfectly to four neighbors, leaving no free electrons to conduct electric current. This makes a silicon crystal an insulator rather than a conductor.In semiconductors i.e. germanium the energy gap is small. Hence at room temperatures some of the valence electrons gain enough thermal energy and jump to the conduction band. More the thermal energy available, the more electrons will get excited and jump to the conduction band. According to the Boltzmann law, the number of thermally excited electrons (n )is given by,n∝e−Eg/KT where Eg is the energy gap for a particular substance, T is the temperature of the substance and K is the Boltzmann constant.If we consider the above equation the number of thermally excited electrons keeps on increasing as we increase the temperature T. If the temperature of the semiconductor is brought down to 0K then the number of thermally excited electrons will literally be nil. Hence there won’t be any electrons in the valence band. Hence we can conclude that at 0K, germanium will behave as an insulator.

questions.text: The crystal of germinium or silicon in its pure form at room temperature acts as:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Insulators are materials that do not conduct electricity well. They have very high resistance to the flow of electric current. Pure germanium or silicon, in its crystalline form at room temperature, does not strictly fall into the category of insulators, as insulators typically have extremely high resistivity. While silicon and germanium have higher resistivity compared to conductors, they are not as insulating as true insulators like rubber or glass.

Conductors are materials that readily allow the flow of electric current. Typically, metals like copper and aluminum are good conductors due to their abundance of free electrons. Pure silicon or germanium, in their crystalline form at room temperature, do not behave as conductors because they do not have a significant number of free electrons available for conduction

This is the correct option. Silicon and germanium are classic examples of semiconductors. At room temperature, they have properties intermediate between those of conductors and insulators. Semiconductors have a small but nonzero conductivity, which increases with temperature. They can be made to conduct much better (as in the case of silicon chips in electronics) by introducing impurities through a process called doping or by applying a voltage.

This option is incorrect because silicon and germanium, in their pure crystalline form at room temperature, do exhibit semi-conductor behavior, as explained above. Therefore, "none of these" is not the correct option.

questions.explanation: At room temperature, pure silicon and germanium would have enough free charge carriers to be classified as a semiconductor. This is because the bandgap energy of a semiconductor is much lower than that of an insulator.

questions.text: All the valence electrons present in a crystal of silicon are bounded in their orbits by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: In a crystal of silicon, each silicon atom forms four covalent bonds with neighboring silicon atoms, creating a crystalline lattice structure. The valence electrons are shared in these covalent bonds, which keep them bounded in their orbits within the crystal lattice.

Ionic bonds involve the transfer of electrons from one atom to another, resulting in the formation of positively and negatively charged ions that are held together by electrostatic attraction. Silicon does not typically form ionic bonds in its crystal structure; instead, it forms covalent bonds.

Molecular bonds involve the sharing of electrons between atoms to form molecules. Silicon does not exist as discrete molecules in a crystal; rather, it forms a continuous lattice structure held together by covalent bonds.
This option is incorrect because silicon in its crystalline form primarily exhibits covalent bonding to hold its structure together.

questions.explanation: As silicon is a non metal all of its valence electrons will form covalent bonds by sharing if electrons to fill their outermost shells and gain stability.

questions.text: Majority charge carries in the P-type region of p-n-junction are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Protons are positively charged particles found in the nucleus of atoms. However, they are not mobile in a semiconductor material like silicon, which forms the basis of P-type regions. Therefore, they are incorrect.

Electrons are negatively charged particles that are the majority carriers in N-type semiconductor regions. In a P-type region, however, electron concentration is significantly lower due to doping with acceptor impurities. Therefore, while some electrons may exist due to thermal excitation, they are not the majority carriers. Thus, this option is incorrect.

Positrons are the antiparticles of electrons, having positive charge. They are not typically present in semiconductor materials and are not relevant in the context of charge carriers in a P-type region. Hence, this option is incorrect.

In a P-type semiconductor, such as silicon doped with acceptor atoms like boron, the majority carriers are "holes." These holes are vacancies in the valence band created by the presence of acceptor impurities. When an electron from a neighboring atom jumps into one of these vacancies, it leaves behind another hole. These holes effectively act as positive charge carriers and contribute to electrical conductivity in the P-type region. Therefore, this is the correct option.

questions.explanation: The majority charge carriers in the P-type region of a p-n junction are "holes."

questions.text: The impurity in the germinium is usually in the ratio of:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Germanium is a semiconductor having an indirect bandgap, as is crystalline silicon. Zone refining techniques have led to the production of crystalline germanium for semiconductors that has an impurity of only one part in 10^8 making it one of the purest materials ever obtained.

questions.text: A potential barrier of 0.7V that exists across p-n junction is made from:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Correct. Silicon is commonly used to make p-n junctions in semiconductor devices like diodes and transistors. When a p-type semiconductor (with an excess of positive "holes") is joined with an n-type semiconductor (with an excess of negative electrons), a depletion region forms at the junction, creating a potential barrier due to the diffusion of charge carriers across it.

Incorrect. Germanium can also be used to make p-n junctions, but silicon is more commonly used in modern semiconductor technology due to its greater abundance, better thermal stability, and higher operating temperatures.

Incorrect. Indium is not typically used to make p-n junctions directly. It is sometimes used in conjunction with other materials for specialized applications, but it is not a primary material for creating p-n junctions.

Incorrect. Gallium is not commonly used to make p-n junctions. While gallium compounds are used in some semiconductor applications (like gallium arsenide in high-speed electronic devices), they are not typically used in standard p-n junctions as silicon or germanium.

questions.explanation: At room temperature, Silicon crystal has fewer free electrons than Germanium , Indium and gallium crystal. This implies that silicon will have much smaller Collector cut off current than that if the others. The variation of Collector cut off current with temperature is less in Silicon compared to Germanium, valium and Indium.

questions.text: A Potential difference is developed across the depletion region of p-n junction due to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: While negative ions are indeed present in the depletion region of a p-n junction (due to the immobile acceptor dopant atoms in the p-region), they alone do not create the potential difference. It's the combined presence of positive and negative ions that contributes to the potential difference

Positive ions are indeed present in the depletion region (due to the immobile donor dopant atoms in the n-region), but again, they alone do not create the potential difference. It's the combined presence of positive and negative ions that contributes to the potential difference.

In a p-n junction, when a depletion region is formed due to the diffusion of majority carriers (holes in the p-region and electrons in the n-region), positive and negative ions are created. These ions result from the immobile dopant atoms left behind as the majority carriers diffuse across the junction. The positive ions are left in the n-region, and the negative ions are left in the p-region.
This option is incorrect because, as explained above, both positive and negative ions are present and contribute to the potential difference across the depletion region of a p-n junction.

questions.explanation: In a p-n junction, when a depletion region is formed due to the diffusion of majority carriers (holes in the p-region and electrons in the n-region), positive and negative ions are created. These ions result from the immobile dopant atoms left behind as the majority of carriers diffuse across the junction. The positive ions are left in the n-region, and the negative ions are left in the p-region.

questions.text: The external pot once applied to p-n junction for forward biasing supplied energy to:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When the p-n junction is forward biased, external voltage is applied such that the positive terminal is connected to the p-side and the negative terminal is connected to the n-side. This causes the free electrons in the n-region to move towards the positive terminal, contributing to the flow of current.
Similarly, the external voltage applied during forward biasing causes the holes in the p-region to move towards the negative terminal, also contributing to the flow of current.
Since both free electrons and holes contribute to the flow of current during forward biasing, option C is correct.

This option is incorrect because both free electrons and holes are involved in the conduction process during forward biasing of a p-n junction. Therefore, it's not accurate to say that none of them are involved.

questions.explanation: When a p-n junction diode is forward biased, i.e. forward biasing voltage V is applied to the diode, this potential difference reduces the barrier potential Vo. The effective barrier potential reduces to (Vo−V)and thickness of the depletion layer also decreases. The holes (majority carriers) in p-region and electrons (majority carriers) in n-junction acquire sufficient energy to cross over the potential barrier across the junction.

questions.text: In forward biased situation, as the biasing voltage is increased the current:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In a forward biased situation, as the biasing voltage is increased, the current through the diode typically increases. This is because forward biasing reduces the potential barrier for the flow of current in a semiconductor diode.

This option is incorrect. In forward biasing, increasing the biasing voltage usually results in an increase in current. The applied voltage helps to overcome the potential barrier, allowing more charge carriers to flow through the diode, thus increasing the current.

This option is correct. When a diode is forward biased, increasing the applied voltage increases the current flowing through it. This is due to the reduction in the depletion region's width and the increase in the number of charge carriers available for conduction, which leads to higher current flow.
This option is incorrect because option C, "Also increases," correctly describes the behavior of current in a forward biased diode as the biasing voltage increases.

questions.explanation: When a diode is forward biased, increasing the applied voltage increases the current flowing through it. This is due to the reduction in the depletion region's width and the increase in the number of charge carriers available for conduction, which leads to higher current flow.

questions.text: While drawing a graph between current and biasing voltage in p-n junction the current is taken:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the x-axis usually represents the biasing voltage (or potential difference), not the current.

This option is incorrect because it's uncommon to plot any physical quantity along the negative y-axis in standard graphs. Additionally, in the context of p-n junctions, current is typically positive and flows in one direction.
This option is correct. The x-axis represents the biasing voltage, and the current is plotted along the y-axis. Expressing current in mA (milliamps) is common for clarity and practicality.

This option is incorrect because it's unusual to plot data along the positive y-axis only. Typically, positive values are plotted above the x-axis, but the current is generally plotted above and below the x-axis to represent both positive and negative values.

questions.explanation: The x-axis represents the biasing voltage, and the current is plotted along the y-axis. Expressing current in mA (milliamps) is common for clarity and practicality.

questions.text: In reverse biased, the resistance offered by the p-n junction is of the order of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In reverse bias, the resistance offered by the p-n junction is typically higher than just a few MΩ. It's in the range of several MΩ to even GΩ (gigaohms), depending on the characteristics of the junction.
This option is correct. When a p-n junction is reverse biased, it acts as a high resistance path for current flow. The resistance is typically several MΩ, as stated.

This option is incorrect. In reverse bias, the resistance is significantly higher than just a few ohms. A few ohms would be characteristic of a forward-biased p-n junction, where it acts more like a conductor.
This option is incorrect. Option B, "Several MΩ," accurately describes the resistance offered by a reverse-biased p-n junction.

questions.explanation: When a p-n junction is reverse biased, it acts as a high resistance path for current flow. The resistance is typically several MΩ.

questions.text: The semi conductor diode has the property of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. Semiconductor diodes allow current to flow in only one direction, from the anode to the cathode, making them capable of one-way conduction. They do not conduct in both directions.

This option is incorrect. Semiconductor diodes, when forward-biased (voltage applied in the correct direction), allow current to flow. When reverse-biased (voltage applied in the opposite direction), they have a small leakage current but are not fully conducting in that direction, so it's not zero conduction.

This option is correct. Semiconductor diodes are primarily used for one-way conduction, allowing current flow in only one direction. This property is essential for rectification, where AC voltage is converted into DC voltage.

This option is incorrect. Semiconductor diodes do not amplify signals. They are primarily used as rectifiers, voltage regulators, and signal demodulators, but they do not provide amplification like transistors or operational amplifiers.

questions.explanation: A semiconductor diode is a device that permits the flow of electric current in only one direction and is made from a silicon or germanium semiconductor material. Semiconductor diode characteristics include unidirectional current flow, a non-linear voltage-current characteristic, and low forward resistance.

questions.text: Electrons present in P-type material due to thermal pair generation are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Majority carriers are the dominant charge carriers in a semiconductor material. In a p-type semiconductor, the majority carriers are the "holes" created due to the absence of electrons in the valence band. Electrons generated by thermal pair generation in a p-type material are not the majority carriers; hence, option a is incorrect.

Minority carriers are the less abundant charge carriers in a semiconductor material. In a p-type semiconductor, the minority carriers are the electrons present in the conduction band. These electrons are generated by thermal pair generation, where some electron-hole pairs are created due to thermal energy. Since these electrons are less abundant compared to the majority carriers (holes), they are considered minority carriers. Therefore, option b is correct.
There is no concept of "dual carriers" in semiconductor physics. It seems to be a made-up term. Thus, option c is incorrect.
This option is incorrect because option b (minority carriers) is correct.

questions.explanation: Electrons are minority charge carriers in p type semiconductors. In an intrinsic semiconductor, some of the electrons in the valence band gain enough energy (thermal energy) to move to the conduction band. So there are a few electrons in the conduction band, and these electrons leave holes in the valence band as they go to the conduction band.

questions.text: Semi conductors with donor atoms and free electrons belong to the type:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Semiconductors with donor atoms (which donate extra electrons) and free electrons belong to the n-type. In n-type semiconductors, the majority charge carriers are electrons.

Semiconductors with acceptor atoms (which accept electrons creating "holes" or lack of electrons) and free holes belong to the p-type. In p-type semiconductors, the majority charge carriers are holes.
This option is incorrect because semiconductors can either be n-type or p-type, not both simultaneously.

This option is incorrect because the classification of semiconductors as n-type or p-type depends on the type of doping introduced (donor atoms for n-type and acceptor atoms for p-type). They cannot belong to both simultaneously.

questions.explanation: N-type semiconductors are created by doping an intrinsic semiconductor with an electron donor element during manufacture. The term n-type comes from the negative charge of the electron. In n-type semiconductors, electrons are the majority carriers and holes are the minority carriers.

questions.text: P -n junction when reversed biased acts as a:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a P-N junction is forward-biased, it acts like a conductor, allowing current to flow easily.When reverse-biased, it doesn't behave like a capacitor. Instead, it blocks the flow of current, unlike a capacitor which stores and releases charge.

A P-N junction, when reversed biased, does not exhibit inductive properties. Inductors store energy in a magnetic field when current flows through them, but the P-N junction in reverse bias does not exhibit such behavior.

This option might seem plausible because a forward-biased P-N junction can act like a closed switch, allowing current to flow. However, in reverse bias, it doesn't function as an "on" switch. It behaves more like an "off" switch because it prevents current flow.

When a P-N junction is reverse biased, it acts as an "off" switch because it effectively blocks the flow of current. This is due to the formation of a depletion region where charge carriers are repelled away from the junction, preventing current flow. Therefore, option d is correct.

questions.explanation: A p-n junction diode acts as an off switch when operating in a reverse-biased region. When a diode or any two-terminal component is connected in a circuit, it has two biased conditions with the given supply: forward bias and reverse bias. In reverse bias, the anode is connected to the negative terminal and the cathode to the positive terminal of the supply. A diode cannot conduct in reverse bias and hence is an open switch.

questions.text: In a n-type semi conductor there are:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: This option is incorrect. In an n-type semiconductor, the majority carriers are electrons, not holes. N-type semiconductors are doped with elements like phosphorus or arsenic, which introduce extra electrons into the crystal lattice, creating an excess of negative charge carriers.

This option is correct. In an n-type semiconductor, while there are mobile electrons as majority carriers, the doping process also introduces immobile positive ions into the crystal lattice. These positive ions are the dopant atoms (e.g., phosphorus or arsenic) that have donated their electrons to the semiconductor crystal, leaving behind positively charged ions.
This option is incorrect. N-type semiconductors don't have immobile negative ions. The immobile ions in an n-type semiconductor are positively charged due to the dopant atoms.
This option is incorrect because option B is correct, as explained above.

questions.explanation: In an N-type semiconductor, the density of negative charge carriers is higher; therefore, it has immobile positive ions, and electrons are the majority charge carriers.

questions.text: The width of depletion region of a junction:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: When a junction is reverse biased, the depletion region widens. This is because the externally applied voltage pushes the majority of carriers away from the junction, leaving behind immobile ions, resulting in an expanded depletion region.

The width of the depletion region is inversely proportional to the doping concentration. Light doping means there are fewer dopant atoms available to create charge carriers, resulting in a wider depletion region. Therefore, this option is incorrect.

Heavy doping means there are more dopant atoms present, which increases the number of charge carriers available. As a result, the depletion region becomes narrower since there are more carriers to neutralize the ionized dopant atoms. Therefore, this option is incorrect.
As explained above, option (a) is correct, making this option incorrect.

questions.explanation: The depletion region width on each side of the p-n junction inversely varies with the dopant concentration. Thus, the depletion width decreases when the dopant concentration is higher and increases when the dopant concentration is lower.

questions.text: Which one of the following has the greatest energy gap:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: CONCEPT:Conductor: The material that allows the electric current to flow through them easily is called a conductor.Example: Metals like -iron, silver, copper, etc.Insulator: The material that doesn't allow the electric current to flow through them is called an insulator.Example: Rubber, wood, etc.Semiconductor: The material having an electric current conducting tendency between conductor and insulator is called a semiconductor.Example: Silicon, germanium, etc.Valence band: The region in which the valence electrons of the atom move is called the valence band.Conduction band: The region in which free electrons remain is called the conduction band.Band Gap: The distance between the valence band of electrons and the conduction band of electrons is known as bandgap.It represents the minimum energy that is required to excite an electron up in the valance band to a state in the conduction band where it can participate in conduction.Conductors have the least band gapwhile insulators have maximum. Semiconductors have a bandgap between conductors and insulators.EXPLANATION:Insulators have the highest bandgapof order 6eV (- 6eV) or more.Semi-Conductors have a bandgap of the order of 1 eV. Conductors have no energy gap (least energy bandgap).This is the reason, they have high conductivity because electrons can move easily from the valance band to the conduction band. Hence the answer will be insulators

questions.text: Which one of the following has smallest energy gap:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: CONCEPT:Conductor: The material that allows the electric current to flow through them easily is called a conductor.Example: Metals like -iron, silver, copper, etc.Insulator: The material that doesn't allow the electric current to flow through them is called an insulator.Example: Rubber, wood, etc.Semiconductor: The material having an electric current conducting tendency between conductor and insulator is called a semiconductor.Example: Silicon, germanium, etc.Valence band: The region in which the valence electrons of the atom move is called the valence band.Conduction band: The region in which free electrons remain is called the conduction band.Band Gap: The distance between the valence band of electrons and the conduction band of electrons is known as bandgap.It represents the minimum energy that is required to excite an electron up in the valance band to a state in the conduction band where it can participate in conduction.Conductors have the least band gap while insulators have maximum. Semiconductors have a bandgap between conductors and insulators.EXPLANATION:Insulators have the highest bandgapof order 6eV (- 6eV) or more.Semi-Conductors have a bandgap of the order of 1 eV. Conductors have no energy gap (least energy bandgap).This is the reason, they have high conductivity because electrons can move easily from the valance band to the conduction band.So the correct answer is option A.

questions.text: Minority carries in n-type substances are:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In the n-type semiconductor, electrons are the majority carriers, and holes the minority carriers

questions.text: Minority carriers in a p-type substances are:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In a p-type semiconductor, the majority carriers are holes, and the minority carriers are electrons

questions.text: A junction between p and n materials forms:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: P-N junction diode is a basic semiconductor device that is widely used in electronic circuits. It is a two-terminal device that allows the flow of current in one direction and blocks it in the opposite direction. The P-N junction diode has many applications, including as a detector, rectifier, and voltage regulator.

questions.text: Semi conductor diode conducts only when it is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: When the diode is forward-biased, the voltage across the diode is in the direction that allows current to flow through it, and the diode conducts. However, when the diode is reverse-biased, the voltage across the diode is in the opposite direction, and the diode does not conduct

questions.text: The forward current through a semi conductor diode circuit is due to:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: The more abundant charge carriers are called majority carriers, which are primarily responsible for current transport in a piece of semiconductor. In n-type semiconductors they are electrons, while in p-type semiconductors they are holes.

questions.text: The reverse current through a semi conductor diode is due to:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: The less abundant charge carriers are called minority carriers; in n-type semiconductors they are holes, while in p-type semiconductors they are electrons. In an intrinsic semiconductor, which does not contain any impurity, the concentrations of both types of carriers are ideally equal.

questions.text: In pn junction, p-type end is basically referred as:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: The diode is a device formed from a junction of n-type and p-type semiconductor material. The lead connected to the p-type material is called the anode and the lead connected to the n-type material is the cathode.

questions.text: In half wave rectification the output DC voltage is obtained acrossthe load for:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The negative halfcycle of input AC
The positive half cycle of input AC

questions.explanation: The half-wave rectifier is a rectifier that allows one-half of the AC voltage signal and blocks the other half, and converts AC voltage to DC voltage, so we obtain the pulsating DC voltage as an output signal. A single diode works for a single-phase supply, but in the case of a three-phase supply, three diodes are needed. Only half of the input waveforms reach the output, so three diodes are needed. By adding more filters in the half-wave rectifier circuit, the rippling of the AC frequency from the output can be reduced.

questions.text: In full wave rectification, the output DC voltage is obtained across the load for:
questions.subject: Physics

options.text: The positive half cycle of input AC
The negative half cycle of input AC

questions.explanation: The input AC supplied to the full wave rectifier is very high. The step-down transformer in the rectifier circuit converts the high voltage AC into low voltage AC. The anode of the centre tapped diodes is connected to the transformer’s secondary winding and connected to the load resistor. During the positive half cycle of the alternating current, the top half of the secondary winding becomes positive while the second half of the secondary winding becomes negative.During the positive half cycle, diode D1 is forward biased as it is connected to the top of the secondary winding while diode D2 is reverse biased as it is connected to the bottom of the secondary winding. Due to this, diode D1 will conduct acting as a short circuit and D2 will not conduct acting as an open circuitDuring the negative half cycle, the diode D1 is reverse biased and the diode D2 is forward biased because the top half of the secondary circuit becomes negative and the bottom half of the circuit becomes positive. Thus in a full wave rectifiers, DC voltage is obtained for both positive and negative half cycle.

questions.text: Forward resistance of p-n junction is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: ⇒ Forward bias is a potential used to reduce the resistance of a PN junction.⇒ A forward biased PN junction has a minimum depletion layer width and junction resistance of few ohms.

questions.text: Reverse resistance of p-n junction is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: ⇒ Reverse bias is a potential used to increase the resistance of a PN junction A reverse-biased PN junction has a maximum depletion layer width and high resistance.

questions.text: Conversion of alternating current into direct current is called:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: CONCEPT:Rectification: A rectifier is a device that converts alternating current (AC) into a direct current (DC). This process is called rectification.A p-n junction can be used as a rectifier because it permits current in one direction only.Amplification: The process of increasing the amplitude of the sound wave is called amplification.In this process, we generally increase the volume of the sound.EXPLANATION:According to the definition of amplification, we increase the amplitude of the sound wave.When we increase the amplitude than the volume of the sound increases.So we can say that the process of increasing the sound volume is called amplification. As in the case of rectification, the AC current is converted into DC. Hence option 4 is correct.

questions.text: A region having zero charge particle:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: When the p-type and the n-type materials are kept in contact with each other, the junction between them behaves differently from either side of the material alone. The electrons and holes are close to each other at the junction. According to coulomb’s law, there is a force between the negative electrons and the positive holes. When the p-n junction is formed a few electrons from the n-type diffuse through the junction and combines with the holes in the p-side to form negative ions and leaves behind positive ions in the n-side. This results in the formation of the depletion layer, which acts as the barrier and does not allow any further flow of electrons from the n region to the p region.

questions.text: The potential difference across depletion region in case of Si is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Typically at room temperature the voltage across the depletion layer for silicon is about 0.7 volts

questions.text: The potential difference across depletion region in case of Ge is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Typically at room temperature the voltage across the depletion region for germanium is about 0.3 – 0.35 volts

questions.text: The most commonly used diode for special purpose is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Special Purpose P-N Junction Diodes includes:Zener DiodeLight-Emitting DiodePhotodiodeA solar cell or photovoltaic devicesLight-emitting diodes, photodiodes, and photovoltaic devices are known as optoelectronic junction devices. Light-Emitting Diode (LED)Light-Emitting Diode is an electric component that emits light when the electric current passes through it. Light-Emitting Diode (LED) is a specific type of diode having similar characteristics as the p-n junction diode. When electricity passes through the LED, the electrons recombine with holes emitting light in the process.The symbol of the diode is shown below. The diode is encased with a clear cover to view the emitted light. The LED can be seen in the picture below.When the LED is forward biased, the electrons flow from the N-type to the P-type. Holes are moved from p to n layer. Due to forward bias, at the boundary, the concentration of the minority carriers increases. At the junction, majority carriers recombine with excess minority carriers. When the minority carriers recombine with the majority carriers, energy is released in the form of photons. Photons with energy less or equal than the bandgap are emitted. The intensity of the light increases when the current reaches maximum.The VI-characteristic of a LED is as shown in the figure below.LEDs can emit red, green, orange, yellow, and blue light. LEDs are widely used as decorative lamps, used in television remote, optical communication, automotive, and display devices.Light-Emitting Diodes require less power to operate. They adapt to instant on-off switching capability. They are economical and offer long-life durability.PhotodiodesPhotodiodes are special p-n junction diodes operated in reverse bias. They are mainly designed for detecting optical signals. Photodiodes feature a transparent window which allows light to fall on it. Photodiodes are also addressed with various names like a light detector, photodetector, and photo-sensor. Silicon, Germanium and Indium gallium arsenide are some of the photodiode materials. The symbol of the photodiode is shown below.The real-time image of the photodiode is as shown below.﻿Photodiodes are made of photosensitive semiconducting material to absorb the light which falls on it. Photodiodes are operated under reverse bias conditions below the breakdown voltage.When the energy of the photons which strike (hv) is greater than the energy gap (Eg) of the semiconductor material in the photodiode, electron-hole pairs are created near the depletion region.Electrons and holes are separated before they combine in the presence of an electric field. When electric current passes through it, electrons are accumulated in the n-side and holes in the p-side. EMF is generated when electrons and holes move in the photodiode. current flows through the device when external load is connected. The magnitude of the photocurrent generated is directly proportional to the intensity of incident light.In reverse bias condition, as the intensity of the light increases, the current also increases. Photodiodes are used in logic circuits, solar cell panels, detection circuits, character recognition circuits and optical communication. Photodiodes are used in smoke detectors and fire detectors.

questions.text: A light emitting diode is made from:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: LEDs are comprised of compound semiconductor materials, which are made up of elements from group III and group V of the periodic table (these are known as III-V materials). Examples of III-V materials commonly used to make LEDs are gallium arsenide (GaAs) and gallium phosphide (GaP).

questions.text: The number of LEDs required to display all the digits is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Light Emitting Diode (LED) is the most widely used semiconductor which emits either visible light or invisible infrared light when forward biased. Remote controls generate invisible light. A Light-emitting diode (LED) is optical-electrical energy into light energy when voltage is applied.Seven segment displays are the output display device that provides a way to display information in the form of images or text or decimal numbers which is an alternative to the more complex dot matrix displays. It is widely used in digital clocks, basic calculators, electronic meters, and other electronic devices that display numerical information. It consists of seven segments of light-emitting diodes (LEDs) which are assembled like numerical 8.

questions.text: A photo diode can be used:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Photodiodes are a class of diodes that converts light energy to electricity. Their working is exactly the opposite of LEDs which are also diodes but they convert electricity to light energy. Photodiodes can also be used in detecting the brightness of the light which is why are used to detect both visible and invisible light.

questions.text: The diode used for the detection of visible and in visible light is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: A photodiode is a semiconductor device that is used to detect light. In order to detect light, a photodiode is exposed to a photon of light and it generates a potential difference upon exposure. It also changes its electrical resistance when it comes into contact with a beam of light

questions.text: A diode, which can turn current ON and OFF in nanosecond is called:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: There are two relevant mechanisms:First, the intended one: Minority carriers, generated by light within or close to the p-n junction and therefore depletion region, reach one of the contacts and therefore are lost for additional transport. This is the contribution, which you can measure externally as generated current or voltage.Second: Photo-generated carriers can also recombine due to scattering mechanisms and recombine with a carrier of opposite sign. Again, this particular carrier pair is lost for future transport. Scattering mechanisms could be crystal defects in the semiconductor material, impurities, phonon-assisted scattering due to elevated temperature, etc. Therefore, every material or heterostructure has some characteristic carrier life time, which determines the shut-off behavior in this case.

questions.text: Photodiode is operated:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: The photodiodes are used in reverse bias condition because the change in reverse current through the photodiode due to change in light flux can be measured easily as the reverse saturation current is directly proportional to the light flux.A photodiode can be regarded as a high-impedance non-ohmic photosensitive device whose current is almost independent of applied voltage. The incident light falls on a reverse-biased semiconductor junction, and the separation of electrons from holes will allow the junction to conduct despite the reverse bias.

questions.text: Photo voltaic cell is also called:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: A solar cell or photovoltaic cell is an electronic device that converts the energy of light directly into electricity by means of the photovoltaic effect. It is a form of photoelectric cell, a device whose electrical characteristics vary when it is exposed to light.

questions.text: A single photovoltaic cell produces a current of:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Individual solar cell devices are often the electrical building blocks of photovoltaic modules, known colloquially as "solar panels". The common single-junction silicon solar cell can produce a maximum open-circuit voltage of approximately 0.6 volts.

questions.text: A single photo voltaic cell produces a current of:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: In general, a typical commercially-available silicon cell produces a current between 28 and 35 milliamps per square centimeter.

questions.text: The light emitting diode emits light when it is:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: An LED is a light emitting diode. The LED emits light when it is forward biased and it emits no light when it is reverse biased. The intensity of light is proportional to the square of the current flowing through the device.Forward bias decreases a diode's resistance, and reverse bias increases a diode's resistance. The current flows effortlessly while in forward bias.

questions.text: The specially designed semi-conductor used for as fast countings in electronic circuits
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Photo diode is linear and has low resistance. Further, it has a very good spectral response. Moreover, its frequency response is also better in addition to having a low dark current.﻿In photodetection, the response time is mainly controlled by the device architecture and electron/hole mobility, while the absorption coefficient and the effective separation of the electrons/holes are the key parameters for high responsivity. Here, we report an approach toward the fast and highly responsive infrared photodetection using an n-type SnSe2 thin film on a p-Si(100) substrate keeping the overall performance of the device. The I–Vcharacteristics of the device show a rectification ratio of ∼147 at ±5 V and enhanced optoelectronic properties under 1064 nm radiation. The responsivity is 0.12 A/W at 5 V, and the response/recovery time constants were estimated as ∼57 ± 25/34 ± 15 μs, respectively. Overall, the response times are shown to be controlled by the mobility of the constituent semiconductors of a photodiode. Further, our findings suggest that n-SnSe2 can be integrated with well-established Si technology with enhanced optoelectronic properties and also pave the way in the design of fast response photodetectors for other wavelengths as well.

questions.text: A combination of p-type and N-type substance give rise to:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: When P-type and N-type come into contact, carriers, which are holes and free electrons, are attracted to each other, recombine at the junction of P-type and N-type, and disappear. Because there are no carriers near the junction, it is called a depletion layer, and it becomes the same state as an insulator.A PN junction hence forms.

questions.text: The semi conductor diode has the property of a:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: A diode is a circuit element that allows electric current to flow in only one direction, like a one-way valve . A diode is created by joining a p-type semiconductor to an n-type semiconductor. The junction between these materials is called a p-n junction. . The positions of the valence and conduction bands are the same, but the impurity levels are quite different. When a p-n junction is formed, electrons from the conduction band of the n-type material diffuse to the p-side, where they combine with holes in the valence band. This migration of charge leaves positive ionized donor ions on the n-side and negative ionized acceptor ions on the p-side, producing a narrow double layer of charge at the p-njunction called the depletion layer. The electric field associated with the depletion layer prevents further The junction between these materials is called a p-n junction. . The positions of the valence and conduction bands are the same, but the impurity levels are quite different. When a p-n junction is formed, electrons from the conduction band of the n-type material diffuse to the p-side, where they combine with holes in the valence band. This migration of charge leaves positive ionized donor ions on the n-side and negative ionized acceptor ions on the p-side, producing a narrow double layer of charge at the p-njunction called the depletion layer. The electric field associated with the depletion layer prevents further diffusion.

questions.text: If positive terminal of the battery is connected to n-type and negative terminal is connected to p-type then diode is:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: By convention, forward biasing of diode is defined in such a way that the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the anode (p-type crystal) of the diode whereas the negative terminal of the battery is connected to the cathode (n-type crystal) of the diode. Moreover when the diode is forward biased then the potential barrier in depletion layer decreases.

If the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the n-type and the negative terminal is connected to the p-type, the diode is said to be reverse biased. In a reverse-biias configuration, the external voltage pulls electrons away from the junction in the n-type and holes away in the p-type, widening the depletion region and preventing current flow (except a very small leakage current).

questions.explanation: If the positive terminal of the battery is connected to the n-type and the negative terminal is connected to the p-type, the diode is said to be reverse biased. In a reverse-biias configuration, the external voltage pulls electrons away from the junction in the n-type and holes away in the p-type, widening the depletion region and preventing current flow (except a very small leakage current).

questions.text: The process due to which current flows only during alternate half cycle is known as:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: For most power applications, half-wave rectification is insufficient for the task. The harmonic content of the rectifier’s output waveform is very large and consequently difficult to filter. Furthermore, the AC power source only supplies power to the load one half every full cycle, meaning that half of its capacity is unused. Half-wave rectification is, however, a very simple way to reduce power to a resistive load. Some two-position lamp dimmer switches apply full AC power to the lamp filament for “full” brightness and then half-wave rectify it for a lesser light output. (figure below)

questions.text: The circuit of full wave rectification consist of:

questions.explanation: Bridge rectifiers are the type of full-wave rectifier that uses four or more diodes in a bridge circuit configuration. Thereby, it can very efficiently convert AC current to DC current. Therefore, the number of diodes found in a bridge rectifier is 4.

questions.text: Transistor was discovered by:

questions.explanation: The transistor was successfully demonstrated on December 23, 1947 atBell Laboratories in Murray Hill, New Jersey. questions.text: In order that a transistor acts as switch, a larger positive potential is applied across its:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: Using a transistor as a switch is the simplest application of the device. A transistor can be extensively used for switching operations, either for opening or closing a circuit. On the other hand, the basic concept behind the operation of a transistor as a switch relies on its mode of operation. Generally, the low-voltage DC is turned on or off by transistors in this mode.Both PNP and NPN transistors can be utilised as switches. A basic terminal transistor can be handled differently from a signal amplifier by biasing both NPN and PNP bipolar transistors with an “ON / OFF” static switch. One of the main uses of the transistor to transform a DC signal “ON” or “OFF” is solid-state switches.Some devices, including LEDs, only require several milliamperes of DC voltages at the logical level and can be directly controlled via the logical gate output. High-power devices such as generators, solenoids or lamps usually need more power to use transistor switches than the usual logic gate..

questions.text: To turn the transistor OFF, the base current is set:
questions.subject: Physics
questions.explanation: With a zero signal applied to the Base of the transistor it turns “OFF” acting like an open switch and zero collector current flows. With a positive signal applied to the Base of the transistor it turns “ON” acting like a closed switch and maximum circuit current flows through the device.

The most common use for capacitors is energy storage, power conditioning, electronic noise filtering, remote sensing and signal coupling/decoupling. Due to the fact that capacitors are an important and versatile function to varied applications, they are used in a wide range of industries.

A transistor is a semiconductor device used to amplify or switch electrical signals and power. It is one of the basic building blocks of modern electronics. It is composed of semiconductor material, usually with at least three terminals for connection to an electronic circuit.

questions.explanation: .A p-n junction diode can work as an excellent rectifier since it offers a low resistance for the current to flow when it is forward biased; but a very high resistance when reverse biased. Thus, it allows current through it only in one direction and acts as a rectifier.

questions.text: Which one of the following devices is based on a p-n junction:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A photodiode contains a p-n junction. It is used to detect light and is operated in the reverse-biased condition. When no light is incident on the junction, the reverse current is almost negligible but when its p-n junction is exposed to light, the reverse current increases with the intensity of light. It is one of the fastest photo-detection devices.

Light-emitting diodes (LED) are made from special semiconductors such as gallium arsenide phosphide in which the potential barrier between p and n sides is such that when an electron combines with a hole during forward bias conduction, a photon of visible light is emitted. They are commonly used as small light sources.

It consists of a thick n-type region covered by a thin p-type layer. When such a p-n junction having no external bias is exposed to light, absorbed photons generate electron-hole pairs. A current flows through the external circuit which is proportional to the intensity of light.
All the above options are examples of devices that make use of p-n junction.

questions.explanation: Photodiode, light emitting diode and photo voltaic cell are all examples of devices in which a p-n junction is used.

The collector and the emitter lie on either side of the base. They have greater concentration of impurity. The collector is comparatively larger than the emitter.

A transistor consists of a single crystal of germanium or silicon which is grown in such a way that it has three regions. The central region is known as base and the other two regions are called emitter and collector.

questions.explanation: A transistor consists of a single crystal of germanium or silicon which is grown in such a way that it has three regions. The central region is known as base and the other two regions are called emitter and collector.

questions.text: In general, most electrical circuits make use of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: An n-n junction refers to a junction between two regions of semiconductor material, both of which are doped with donor impurities, creating an excess of negative charge carriers (electrons). This type of junction is uncommon in traditional semiconductor devices but can be found in specialized applications, such as certain types of sensors or photodetectors.

A p-p junction typically refers to a junction between two regions of semiconductor material, both of which are doped with acceptor impurities, creating an excess of positive charge carriers (holes). This type of junction is not commonly encountered in standard semiconductor devices but can have specialized applications in certain types of sensors or photodetectors. However, it's worth noting that the more common junctions encountered in semiconductor devices are p-n junctions, where one region is doped with acceptor impurities (p-type) and the other with donor impurities (n-type).
This is the correct option as n-p-n transistors are used most often in actual practice.
In actual practice, n-p-n transistors are generally used.

questions.explanation: An n-p-n transistor is one of the most common types of bipolar junction transistors (BJTs). It consists of three semiconductor layers: a thin p-type semiconductor layer sandwiched between two n-type semiconductor layers. The middle layer acts as the base, while the outer layers serve as the emitter and collector regions. In an n-p-n transistor, the majority carriers in the emitter and collector regions are electrons (n-type), while the base region has majority carriers of holes (p-type). The flow of current through the transistor is controlled by the voltage applied to the base relative to the emitter. N-p-n transistors are widely used in amplification and switching circuits in electronic devices.

questions.text: For a normal transistor, the emitter current can be given by:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A small part of the emitter current flows out of the base (IB). So, this option is incorrect.

After the application of the biasing voltage to the transistor, the emitter-base junction is forward-biased, so the emitter injects a large number of electrons into the base region. These free electrons in the base can flow in either of two directions. They can either flow out of the base to the positive terminal of VBB (the base-biased voltage which forward-biased the emitter-base junction) or they can be attracted towards the collector because of battery VCC( current collecting voltage which reverse-biased the collector-base junction). In a normal biased transistor, an electronic current IE flows from the emitter into the base. A very small part of it, the current IB, flows out of the base, and the rest of it IC flows out of the collector. Hence, we can say that IE = IC + IB.
A very small part of the emitter current, current IB, flows out of the base. The rest of it flows out of the collector so, this option is incorrect.

After the application of the biasing voltage to the transistor, the emitter-base junction is forward-biased, so the emitter injects a large number of electrons into the base region. These free electrons in the base can flow in either of two directions. They can either flow out of the base to the positive terminal of VBB(the base-biased voltage which forward-biased the emitter-base junction) or they can be attracted towards the collector because of battery VCC( current collecting voltage which reverse-biased the collector-base junction). In a normal biased transistor, an electronic current IE flows from the emitter into the base. A very small part of it, the current IB, flows out of the base, and the rest of it IC flows out of the collector. Hence, we can say that IE = IC + IB.

questions.explanation: After the application of the biasing voltage to the transistor, the emitter-base junction is forward-biased, so the emitter injects a large number of electrons into the base region. These free electrons in the base can flow in either of two directions. They can either flow out of the base to the positive terminal of VBB (the base-biased voltage which forward-biased the emitter-base junction) or they can be attracted towards the collector because of battery VCC( current collecting voltage which reverse-biased the collector-base junction). In a normal biased transistor, an electronic current IE flows from the emitter into the base. A very small part of it, the current IB, flows out of the base, and the rest of it IC flows out of the collector. Hence, we can say that IE = IC + IB.

questions.text: The open loop gain of an op-amplifier is of the order of:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The main function of an operational amplifier is to amplify the input signal and the more open loop gain it has the better. Open-loop gain is the gain of the op-amp without positive or negative feedback and for such an amplifier the gain will be infinite but typical real values range from about 20,000 to 200,000.

b) 10^6:This option suggests that the open-loop gain of an op-amp is of the order of 10^6. Again, this value is within the typical range for the open-loop gain of many op-amps. Higher gain values allow for greater amplification of input signals.

c) 10^-5:This option suggests that the open-loop gain of an op-amp is of the order of 10^-5. However, it is unusual for the open-loop gain of an op-amp to be expressed with a negative exponent. Op-amps typically have positive gain values.

d) 10^-6:This option suggests that the open-loop gain of an op-amp is of the order of 10^-6. Similar to option c, a negative exponent is uncommon for expressing the open-loop gain of an op-amp. Positive gain values, often in the order of 10^5 or 10^6, are more typical.

questions.explanation: The open-loop DC gain (usually referred to as AVOL and sometimes as forward gain) is the gain of the amplifier without the feedback loop being closed, hence the name “open-loop.”

questions.text: Question is given below:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: The cross product of two parallel vectors is zero because the sine of the angle between them is zero. Hence, this option is correct.i × i = |i||i| sin(0) = 0
This option is incorrect. The magnitude of the cross product depends on the sine of the angle between the vectors. Since the vectors are parallel, the angle is zero, making the cross product zero.
This option is incorrect. The cross product calculation involves the sine of the angle between the vectors, which is zero for parallel vectors, resulting in a cross product of zero.
This option is incorrect. The cross product of two parallel vectors results in zero because the sine of zero degrees is zero.

questions.explanation: The cross product of two parallel vectors is always zero because the sine of the angle between them is zero. In this case, i × i = |i||i| sin(0) = 0. This means that when dealing with parallel vectors, the resulting cross product will always be zero, as seen in Option A. Options B, C, and D are incorrect because they do not consider the fact that parallel vectors have a sine of 0, resulting in a cross product of 0.

questions.text: The p-n junction on forward biasing acts as a:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: A p-n junction acts as a capacitor when it's reverse-biased. In this state, the depletion region widens, creating a potential barrier that behaves like a dielectric between the p and n regions, effectively fo
When a p-n junction is forward-biased, it allows the current to flow across it. It behaves as a resistor in the circuit and offers a resistance of only a few ohms when conducting.
Inductors are typically formed by coils of wire or other conductive materials, not by p-n junctions.
The resistance offered by a p-n junction is very high when it is reverse biased.

questions.explanation: When forward-biased, a p-n junction allows a current of a few milliamperes to flow across it because there is sufficient energy to overcome the potential barrier. It offers a low resistance of only a few ohms in this state.

questions.text: Temperature coefficient of resistivity of a semi-conductor:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: For semiconductors, the temperature coefficient of resistivity is negative because when the temperature of a semiconductor is increased its resistance decreases. So, the conductivity of the semiconductor material increases with an increase in temperature.

The temperature coefficient of resistivity is positive for conductors because when the temperature of a conductor is increased, the amplitude of vibration of the atoms in the lattice increases and hence, the probability of their collision with free electrons also increases. So, the resistance to the flow of electrons increases.

For semiconductors, the temperature coefficient of resistivity is negative because when the temperature of a semiconductor is increased its resistance decreases. So, the conductivity of the semiconductor material increases with an increase in temperature.

For semiconductors, the temperature coefficient of resistivity is negative because when the temperature of a semiconductor is increased its resistance decreases. So, the conductivity of the semiconductor material increases with an increase in temperature.

questions.explanation: The temperature coefficient of resistance of a semiconductor is negative. It means that resistance decreases with the increase in temperature. This results in a higher number of charge carriers available for recombination, increasing the conductivity of the semiconductor.

questions.text: Which one of the following is not a semi conductor:
questions.subject: Physics

options.explanation: Silicon is a semiconductor.
Copper is a metal. Hence, it is a conductor so this is the correct option.
Gallium arsenide is a special semiconductor material used in making light-emitting diodes.
Germanium is another semiconductor material.

questions.explanation: Copper is a metal and all metals are conductors because they have free electrons. Silicon, germanium and gallium arsenide are semi-conductors and they only allow current to flow under specific conditions.
